scope: security slice A — secrets / credentials lifecycle
verdict_rationale: Zero CRITICAL findings; one MAJOR is mitigated by documented policy semantics and the default of 'refuse', and remaining items are MINOR / INFO observations about heuristic precision and operator-visibility gaps.
detailed_analysis: The secret/credential lifecycle is implemented around a single classifier (mcp_secret_policy.py) that fires at two boundaries: parse (mcp_server_io/parse.py:49) and render (mcp_server_io/render.py:60). The classifier is structurally sound: find_mcp_secret_literals walks the parsed dict structurally rather than touching raw bytes; path elements correctly distinguish dict keys from list indices; NFKC+casefold normalization is applied so Unicode-confusable field names cannot bypass SECRET_FIELD_RE. Default policy is `refuse`. apply_mcp_secret_policy deep-copies its input before classifying and mutating, so there is no TOCTOU between detection and redaction. Env-reference handling round-trips correctly. The CLI export/import paths do not introduce a new boundary: import_from_zip validates every zip member's pair_id against canonical UUIDv4 via validate_pair_id BEFORE using it as a filesystem component, foreclosing zip-slip. YAML loading is ruamel.yaml.YAML(typ='rt') — round-trip mode does not execute Python tags. TOML uses Python's stdlib tomllib. State persistence (state.json) carries no secret-bearing fields by construction. Logging is restrained. Privacy gate fails CLOSED on read or parse failure. One structural concern (MAJOR): under mcp_server_secret_policy = permissive, literal secrets are persisted into the canonical (and therefore into the export zip and into every projected tool's slot file) — neither the CLI export subcommand nor the export zip's manifest carries an explicit marker that the snapshot may contain secrets. Two MINOR observations: heuristic precision on low-entropy prefix + env reference in known-secret fields, and partial slot bytes leaking into tomllib decoder error tracebacks via logging.exception. Nothing reaches CRITICAL.

findings.category: Secret persistence under operator-selected permissive policy without export-time guard
findings.description: export_to_zip reads canonicals via load_canonical() and writes them verbatim into the snapshot zip without re-applying the secret policy. If the user configured mcp_server_secret_policy = permissive, every literal secret that was permitted at parse time is sitting in <state_dir>/canonical/<pair_id>.json and is included in the exported zip in cleartext. A subsequent import_from_zip on a different host also accepts those canonicals without re-classification. The CLI emits no warning at export time that the snapshot may carry secret material, and the manifest carries no contains_secret_literals flag. Mitigations: default policy is refuse; under redact, the canonical contains only placeholder env-var names; under permissive, the per-poll warning is logged. The risk is confined to the explicit operator opt-in.
findings.exploit_scenario: User A enables permissive to onboard a legacy MCP server config with a literal api_key. They sync, then run agents-sync export library.zip to share their library. User B receives library.zip over a chat channel; the API key is now leaked into the chat archive and B's local <state_dir>/canonical/.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) re-apply apply_mcp_secret_policy at export time with the active policy and refuse to export when literals would otherwise pass through under permissive (gate behind --allow-secrets), or (b) classify each canonical at export time and set a contains_secret_literals: true flag in the manifest plus a CLI WARNING line listing affected pair_ids. Option (a) is the surgical fix; option (b) preserves the permissive workflow but makes the leak visible at egress.

findings.category: Heuristic precision — known-secret field with low-entropy prefix + env reference treated as safe
findings.description: is_safe_secret_reference strips every env-reference token from the value and declares the result safe if the literal remainder does not match HIGH_CONFIDENCE_SECRET_VALUE_RE. Because _is_secret_literal consults this function BEFORE checking SECRET_FIELD_RE against the key name (line 266), a value like 'XYZ-${env:TOKEN}' in a field named api_key will be classified as not-a-secret.
findings.exploit_scenario: A user pastes 'org-12345-abc-${env:TOKEN}' into a connection_string field. The 'org-12345-abc-' portion is sensitive (tenant identifier + partial credential) but does not match any provider regex. Under refuse, the value sails through and the literal prefix lands in every tool's slot file.
findings.recommendation: When the field name matches SECRET_FIELD_RE (or the path lands in headers.authorization / headers.x-api-key), require the ENTIRE value to be an env reference, not merely 'contains an env ref with no high-confidence literal remainder'.

findings.category: Partial slot bytes leaking into logged tracebacks via tomllib decoder errors
findings.description: logging.exception at enumerator.py:101 and :174 dumps a full traceback when extract_pair_id raises. For TOML slots, the raised tomllib.TOMLDecodeError includes column/line context that can carry portions of the slot text — including secret-bearing field values — into the operator's log stream.
findings.exploit_scenario: An MCP server config in Codex config.toml has a malformed quoted string adjacent to an api_key = '...' line. Discovery reads the file, tomllib raises TOMLDecodeError referencing the line; logging.exception writes the traceback to stderr. If logs are shipped to a centralised collector, the partial secret bytes follow.
findings.recommendation: Replace logging.exception(...) with logging.error('Cannot extract pair_id from %s %s slot: path=%s slot=%s (%s)', tool_name, ctype, path, slot_key, type(exc).__name__) and capture the full traceback at DEBUG level only.

findings.description: If a user runs with permissive for a period, then tightens to refuse, previously-written canonicals still contain literal secrets. Subsequent parse attempts on the source slot will raise McpSecretLeakError, so the canonical is never rewritten — the stale literal sits on disk indefinitely. export_to_zip would still pick it up.
findings.exploit_scenario: Operator tightens policy after a security review. They believe their canonicals are clean. They later export a snapshot; the snapshot still contains material from the permissive period.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) on Syncer.sync_once, scan every loaded canonical with find_mcp_secret_literals and apply the active policy in-place; or (b) provide an agents-sync canonical-rescan CLI subcommand that walks <state_dir>/canonical/ and re-applies the active policy to each entry.

findings.category: Permissive-mode warning visibility — single WARNING-level line per poll
findings.description: Under permissive, the warning is emitted at WARNING with field paths. The cache de-duplicates per (artifact, fields) tuple within a poll. There is no escalation if the same secret persists across N consecutive polls, nor a metric/counter the operator can scrape.
findings.exploit_scenario: Operator-visibility issue. A user enables permissive temporarily, forgets to revert, and the secret material continues to flow through canonicals and projected tool files.
findings.recommendation: Add a periodic ERROR-level escalation every N polls or every M minutes the permissive condition has held. Document in README that permissive is intended for short debugging windows only.
